body.u: I had just left school. The fact that I didn’t have to go back hadn’t quite clicked into place yet.
That July was beutifullybeautifully sunny and I didn’t bother about preparing for my interval in August.
Eventually, August came and I got down to preparing for my interview.

The day came, I walked through the door and sat down in a chair next to about ten other aplicantsapplicants. I was confident of breaking the ice and at least getting some “height off the ground” but after that I faded away.
I got the job at least and liked it very much at first. I was determined to not get bored to quickly, so I varied my way of going about it.
But as all jobs go, because you are compelled to do it, your thoughts just to fade, on the thing you used to like so much.
After two years at the same job I had found a better opportunity and took days off to find out the details.
After deciding I’d like to try for it I prepared for the interview.
Like many before I wasn’t aceptedaccepted. I still liked the type of work so I tryedtried a different approach and it showed possibilities.
I waited for a month and then decided they’d given me up.
Eventually I found a better job and got it, but it wasn’t easy.
Being a new job I was quickly enthused and liked it very much.
It didn’t pay much, but I didn’t need much and I was contented for about five years. It was on that 5th year that I met my life long companion.
We were married in the following year 1986. I was 24 and pleased with myself for not getting hitched at an early age.
With family planning and everything, I knew I had to get another job which paid more than I was getting.
I searched, went to interviews, and slogged hard at overtime, to keep going. Eventually I got a job. It was well paid, but really, it wasn’t my sort of work.
I didn’t think that would matter because they were fairly short hours and the money made a difference.
But I was wrong. I managed to grin and bear it, but that didn’t last long. I decided in going for another interview.
Only one came through and because of various things happening at home, I didn’t prepare and didn’t get the job.
Anyway, I learned to like it and I had that job up to the day I retired and I don’t think I regretted that much. I certainly don’t now.
